text: Okay, so as you know we are going up the OSI model layers and we are at Layer 2, the data link. Okay, so as you know we are going up the OSI model layers and we are at Layer 2, the data link layer, and there are some significant protocols that operate at Layer 2 that we can think of from a security perspective. Now if you remember back in the old days when the only way to get into a network remotely was to use dial up lines and modems, the industry had a problem. The problem was how do we allow TCP IP to run across dial up lines? So the industry had to come up with a protocol that would allow TCP IP and its protocols to run across dial up line. As you know dial up lines are analog connections and so they had to create a protocol that would allow that and what they came up with is something called slip. Serial line interface protocol is what that stands for. Now eventually when remote access became really popular they decided to replace this protocol with something better because as companies started to offer remote access to everyone and it became a necessity for all organizations. They decided to replace that older slip protocol with something that is much better and what they called it is what you see here PPP point to point protocol. Now by the time they did this they realized that a lot of companies were offering remote access and what they should do with this protocol because it was going to be used for remote access is build some authentication protocols right into this PPP protocol because they realized that this would be used by people that would be connecting in remotely by using for example VPNs and so they decided to build three authentication protocols as part of the PPP authentication protocol itself or remote access protocol itself and they gave us three instead of one and the reason that there are three is because they all provide different levels of security as you'll see and this is something that's important for you to focus on. The first one the first authentication protocol that they built natively into PPP is called PAP password authentication protocol. This one is the simplest but it's also the least secure. In fact what this uses is a plain text reusable static password in order to authenticate the individual at the other end that's trying to connect remotely. Now if you think about those three words that I just use plain text reusable static those are not good security words but that's all that PAP supports and so what it does is it sends your password across the line in plain text so if there's anybody in between like there is likely to be they can see your password and here's the real problem we can never change that password you cannot force a password change so PAP is not very good and you wonder why did we even have it but it is supported by a lot of simple network devices and in fact in the early days of remote access this was really the only option for people and companies to offer remote access and it's still widely used by many applications. What's better is the second one it's called CHAP challenge handshake authentication protocol. What this one does is it addresses some of the problems that EAP does or has and so when we send the password across the line using CHAP it is encrypted and you can force people to change passwords every 30 days or every 60 days or whatever it is that the company decides on and so it's a much more secure protocol in fact it is very popular out there even today for organizations that offer remote access through VPNs because ultimately this person sitting behind that VPN needs to be authenticated and a lot of companies have standardized on the CHAP protocol as part of for example their radius and tax and diameter administration systems that companies will implement in order to host remote access but CHAP much better and in fact it also supports two factor authentication which has become pre-standard for organizations to use in order to allow remote access. The best one is actually the third one and it's called EAP and the reason it's the best is because of the E the E stands for extensible and so it can be adaptable it can be changed so almost address anything that an organization might want from a remote access perspective so that the way that they built EAP is that it's very flexible you can use it in conjunction with almost anything else and so you see it a lot today in any service that requires remote access in fact the next slide here gives us an idea of EAP and how good it is there is one version of it that you would be wise to understand as we have seen it appear on the exam and that is P EAP and the P is actually for protected EAP and so what they do with this one is they protect the EAP traffic across a SSL and TLS tunnel in other words the EAP traffic that contains your authentication information is actually protected by using the SSL slash TLS protocol which provides authentication and encryption and possibly some other things so remember P EAP which stands for protected EAP simply tunnels the EAP traffic across a SSL slash TLS tunnel but here's why EAP is so extensible there are many versions of it that the industry has come up with to really suit any remote access capability that any organization or service might require so we've got EAP TLS we've got EAP T TLS and we've got EAP P EAP which we just talked about we've got lightweight EAP as well and they all provide as you can see here different levels of allowing remote entities to authenticate through the EAP protocol but do it in such a way so that it becomes very efficient for the organization that's hosting the remote access requirements there's even a EAP MD5 which really addresses the integrity of that traffic as it goes through for example the EAP authentication information even though we've provided a really good slide here probably the one that is the most important for you to remember is P EAP that we've talked about just before now we're still at layer 2 and what we need to understand at layer 2 are some of the significant devices and protocols that operate at layer 2 of the OSI model and as far as devices are concerned really two of them that are significant bridges and switches both operate at layer 2 and therefore because they operate at layer 2 they are still very efficient but they are really dumb devices in other words we can't really ask any of these devices to make any security decisions they just don't have the intelligence to be able to do that but both bridges and switches are layer 2 devices now don't overthink these questions because as you know today companies can buy layer 3 switches they can buy layer 4 switches you can even buy layer 7 switches but unless the exam specifically mentions those words you need to remember that a switch natively operates at layer 2 and they are both bridges and switches dumb devices in fact that's why we call them bridges they simply bridge two things together and that bridge does not care what's going across it it has no ability to interpret it so these are very fast devices but we cannot use them to make any sort of security decisions switches have a little more functionality than bridges do because of the ports that are available at the back of a switch and so if you connect devices to the ports that are at the back of the switch it starts giving us the ability to start creating what are called segments or virtual lands as we will discuss later when we get into the partitioning and segmentation aspect of network security now these are some of the other layer 2 protocols that are noteworthy at layer 2 and you'll know that some of these are layer 2 for sure because they are called L2 L2 TP obviously the L2 stands for layer 2 but PPTP also a layer 2 tunneling protocol we just described what slip was that older protocol that was used for remote access that was eventually replaced by the PPP protocol that contains those authentication protocols that we just talked about PAP, CHAP and EAP also at this layer is what we call ARP and reverse ARP and those are some significant protocols that operate at layer 2 what ARP does is it maps IP addresses which is the logical addressing that's used across networking to specific MAC addresses which is what makes you unique on any network and so as something comes in as an IP address the network has to take that and map it to the correct MAC address and that's what ARP does now when we originate a outgoing request from the internal network it obviously originates from your MAC address but that has to be converted to the correct IP address which is what reverse ARP does but both ARP and reverse ARP are layer 2 protocols that work basically together now L2F PPTP L2TP or what we call tunneling protocols tunneling protocols simply take something and encapsulate them within something else and we'll learn later that this is the precursor for how we create and use VPNs because VPNs require tunneling first and then encryption so these protocols that you see here L2F PPTP L2TP are layer 2 tunneling protocols and because they operate at layer 2 they are still very efficient but they may lack in functionality as you know the lower down the layers we go the less we can do but the more efficient we become the higher up you go the more we can do the more functional we become but the less efficient we become so these are very efficient tunneling protocols that operate at layer 2 but may be limited in some of the functionality that they can offer
